fact: August 9 is exactly 221 days into the year, which means there are only 144 days left until New Year's Eve!
In the Northern Hemisphere, this is prime time for cicadas to buzz so loudly they can reach 120 decibels - that's louder than a chainsaw!
The sun rises around 6 AM and sets around 8 PM in most of North America on August 9, giving you about 14 hours of daylight to play outside.
August 9 falls smack in the middle of Leo season, when the zodiac lion is roaring at full strength until August 22.
Perseid meteor showers peak right around this date every year, so you might see up to 60 shooting stars per hour if you stay up late!
In Australia and other Southern Hemisphere countries, August 9 is deep winter, and some places might have their coldest temperatures of the year.
Sunflowers in the Northern Hemisphere are at their most gigantic around this time, with some growing taller than basketball players!
The moon phase on August 9 changes every year, but when it's a new moon on this date, the night sky becomes extra dark and perfect for stargazing.
August 9 is roughly 165 days away from the winter solstice in the north, making it almost the exact opposite time of year from the shortest day.
In many places, August 9 marks when morning dew starts getting heavier as nights begin their slow journey toward becoming longer than days.
Crickets chirp faster in the heat around this date - you can actually count their chirps for 15 seconds, add 37, and get the temperature in Fahrenheit!
August 9 sits right in the 'dog days of summer,' a period that ancient people believed made dogs go crazy from the heat.
